evals.prompt: Write the base CSS theme tokens for a site that supports both light and dark mode using the modern color system.
evals.expected_output: Declares color-scheme: light dark; on :root, uses light-dark() for theme tokens, and keeps prefers-color-scheme only as fallback or a specialized override.
expectations: Theme tokens are defined as CSS custom properties
color-scheme is declared on :root
light-dark() is used for token values
prefers-color-scheme appears only as a fallback or specialized override

evals.prompt: Add an adaptive button, badge, tag, and status color pattern using contrast-color() with a fallback.
evals.expected_output: Uses contrast-color() behind @supports for dynamic component foregrounds, with explicit fallback colors and practical component examples.
expectations: Adaptive components use contrast-color()
The contrast-color() usage is behind an @supports check
Buttons, badges, tags, or status components are included
Fallback colors are still present

evals.prompt: Explain the accessibility cautions around contrast-color() and how to test modern CSS color functions.
evals.expected_output: States that contrast-color() returns only black or white today, warns that mid-tone colors can still be problematic, and requires manual WCAG contrast and browser support testing.
expectations: The output explains that contrast-color() is not a replacement for testing
The output says contrast-color() returns black or white only
The output warns about mid-tone background colors
The output mentions manual WCAG contrast checks

evals.prompt: Write a progressive enhancement fallback for browsers that do not support light-dark() or contrast-color().
evals.expected_output: Uses @supports and prefers-color-scheme fallbacks so unsupported browsers still get readable colors without JavaScript.
expectations: The response uses @supports for modern CSS color functions
The response keeps a prefers-color-scheme fallback
The response does not require JavaScript for theming
The response keeps readable fallback colors

evals.prompt: Write a compact testing checklist for a theme that uses color-scheme, light-dark(), contrast-color(), and forced-colors.
evals.expected_output: Covers browser support, light and dark mode verification, forced-colours and high contrast mode, and manual WCAG contrast checks.
expectations: Browser support is checked
Light mode and dark mode are checked
Forced-colours and high contrast are checked
Manual WCAG contrast validation is included

evals.prompt: Build a three-option theme selector with System, Light, and Dark using native buttons and aria-pressed.
evals.expected_output: Uses a labelled group with three native button elements, aria-pressed for selection state, and no arrow-key navigation between options.
expectations: Uses native button elements with type="button"
Uses aria-pressed="true" for selected option
Uses a labelled group (role="group" with aria-label)
Does NOT use role="radio" or role="radiogroup"

evals.prompt: Write JavaScript that separates the user's selected mode from the resolved theme, storing system/light/dark but rendering only light or dark.
evals.expected_output: Maintains selectedMode and resolvedTheme as separate values, applies both as data-theme-mode and data-theme attributes, and listens for prefers-color-scheme changes when system is active.
expectations: Separates selected mode from resolved theme
Uses data-theme-mode for user selection
Uses data-theme for rendered scheme
Listens for prefers-color-scheme changes

evals.prompt: Write an early initialization script that applies the stored theme before first paint to prevent colour flashing.
evals.expected_output: Reads localStorage safely with try/catch, validates the stored value, defaults to system, resolves system using prefers-color-scheme, and sets root attributes before DOMContentLoaded.
expectations: Runs before DOMContentLoaded
Uses try/catch for localStorage
Validates stored values
Sets data-theme-mode and data-theme on documentElement

evals.prompt: Write CSS that makes a theme selector work in Windows High Contrast Mode with visible button boundaries and distinct selected state.
evals.expected_output: Uses forced-colors media query with system colors, ensures button borders remain visible, and distinguishes selected from focused state without relying on background colour alone.
expectations: Uses @media (forced-colors: active)
Uses system color keywords
Button boundaries remain visible
Selected state is identifiable

evals.prompt: Document the failure pattern where storing dark when the user selected system and the OS happened to be dark causes incorrect persistence.
evals.expected_output: Explains that when the user selects system, the stored value must be system, not the resolved dark/light value, so that future OS changes continue to affect the page.
expectations: Identifies the incorrect persistence pattern
Explains why storing the resolved value is wrong
Shows the correct approach (storing system)
must_not_contain: storing dark when system is dark is correct
